content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Pat Meyering 
310 W. Duane Av. 
Sunnyvale, CA 94085 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-148 
Dear Mr. Meyering: 
September 8, 2016 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the 
conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions 
such as common law conflict of interest or Section l 090. 
QUESTION 
Do the Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit you from taking part in City Council 
decisions concerning the Peery Park Specific Plan and Final Environmental Impact Report based on 
your ownership of a residence in close proximity to the project area at issue? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. As described below, the Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibits you from taking 
part in City Council decisions concerning the Peery Park Specific Plan and Final Environmental 
Impact Report based on your ownership of a residence in close proximity to the project area at 
issue. 
FACTS 
You are a member of the Sunnyvale City Council. On or after August 31, 2016, the City 
Council is going to consider and vote on a Final Environmental Impact Report ("EIR") and Specific 
Plan to increase density of development allowed for a 450-acre portion of the city described as 
"Peery Park." 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations, All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-16-148 
Page No. 2 
According to the EIR, there is an anticipated demand for 2.2 million square feet of net new 
development under the Peery Park Specific Plan that is driven by growth in the technology sector 
and a significant demand for office space.2 Moreover, the intent underlying the plan is to: 
• Create a high-tech 21st century employment center within the City of 
Sunnyvale. 
• Improve the visual characteristics of Peery Park through architectural, 
landscaping, and pedestrian-oriented improvements. 
• Support and attract high-profile technology finns. 
• Develop activity centers to provide commercial and recreational 
opportunities for residents and employees, and alleviate over-use of existing 
commercial and recreational facilities. 
• Strengthen and provide opportunities for small-scale technology firms. 
• Provide opportunities to develop housing in a small portion of the Project 
area to create a transition between existing residential neighborhoods and the 
commercial and industrial uses within Peery Park in a manner that would 
protect the privacy and security of existing residents. 
• Improve multi-modal accessibility for parking and transportation to Peery 
Park, including a more pedestrian and bicycle friendly environment to reduce 
and improve the circulation of vehicle traffic within Peery Park. 
You own a home on Duane A venue in Sunnyvale. According to your facts, the closest 
distance from the initial Peery Park Specific Plan area to your home was 500 feet. However, in the 
last 8 years, the Specific Plan area has changed to include certain lots that are within 500 feet of 
your home. 
A sound wall that is almost 12 feet tall sits between the residential area where your home is 
located and all of the land included in the Specific Plan area. The sound wall establishes a solid 
perimeter around your neighborhood. As a result, the distance from your home to the closest portion 
of the Specific Plan area is 4,755 feet by walking, biking or driving. 
ANALYSIS 
The Act's conflict of interest rules prohibits a public official from making, participating in 
making, or using his or her official position in any way to influence a governmental decision in 
which the official knows, or has reason to know, that he or she has a "financial interest." (Section 
87100.) A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87103 
if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable 
from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her immediate family, or 
on any one of five enumerated economic interests. (Section 87103.) 
In the present matter, the pertinent interest is real property in which you have an interest of 
$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b}.) 
. We reviewed portions of the EIR referenced in your request at http;//sunnyvale.ca.gov/. 


Foreseeability and Materiality 
File No. A-16-148 
Page No. 3 
A financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable when it is explicitly involved in 
a decision. Financial interests that are explicitly involved include an interest that is a named party 
in, or subject of, a governmental decision. (Regulation 1870 l (a).) Your interest is not explicitly 
involved in the governmental decision at issue. For interests that are not explicitly involved, as here, 
the effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. "In general, if the financial 
effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is 
reasonably foreseeable." (Regulation 1870l(b).) 
For real property not explicitly involved in a decision and pertinent to your facts, a 
foreseeable effect is material if the decision: 
( 10) Would change the character of the parcel of real property by 
substantially altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including 
parking, of property surrounding the official's real property parcel, the 
view, privacy, noise levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other 
factors that would affect the market value of the real property parcel in 
which the official has a financial interest; 
( 11) Would consider any decision affecting real property value located 
within 500 feet3 of the property line of the official's real property ... 
Notwithstanding this prohibition, the Commission may provide written 
advice allowing an official to participate under these circumstances if 
the Commission determines that there are sufficient facts to indicate 
that there will be no reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the 
official's property; 
(12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and 
consideration under the circumstances, to believe that the 
governmental decision was of such a nature that its reasonably 
foreseeable effect would influence the market value of the official's 
property. 
(Regulation 18702.2(a)( 10)-( 12).} 
As mentioned, Peery Park encompasses a 450-acre portion of the city where there is an 
anticipated demand for 2.2 million square feet of new development with the concept that it will 
evolve into a "high-tech 21st century employment center." The new development will improve the 
"visual characteristics of Peery Park through architectural, landscaping, and pedestrian-oriented 
improvements." In addition, plans for the project area include transportation improvements, activity 
centers for commercial and recreational purposes, as well as "housing in a small portion of the 
project area to create a transition between existing residential neighborhoods and the commercial 
3 We have consistently advised that the measurement is from the nearest boundary of the project to the nearest 
boundary of the official's property. The presence of a wall that surrounds your neighborhood and increases driving 
distance to the project is not relevant to the application of the "500.foot rule." 


File No. A-16-148 
Page No. 4 
and industrial uses within Peery Park in a manner that would protect the privacy and security of 
existing residents." And all of these improvements will occur in an area adjacent to the residential 
neighborhood where your home is currently located. 
Taking all of these facts into consideration, there is little doubt that a reasonably prudent 
person would have reason to believe that the governmental decision concerning the Peery Park EIR 
and Specific Plan will influence the market value of your home. A project of this magnitude that 
intends, among other things, to add a substantial amount high-end office space dubbed a "high-tech 
21st century employment center" will most certainly increase the demand for housing in close 
proximity to the project area, including your current neighborhood, thus increasing your home's 
value."1 
We also note your statement that the distance from the initial Peery Park Specific Plan area 
to your home was 500 feet or more, and only within the last 8 years has the Project area included 
certain lots that are actually within 500 feet of your home. The conflict of interest analysis considers 
the facts at the time of a decision Thus, the prior formulation of the plan would not be relevant. 
In addition, you asked whether se&fJllentation of the decision into two separate votes (the 
original Peery Park project area and the later added portion of the project area) would allow you to 
vote on the original Peery Park project area. However, our conclusion is the same regardless of 
which portion of the project area is at issue since these decisions are interlinked5 and not 
segmentable. Thus, our conclusion is that you have a prohibitory conflict of interest with respect to 
the entire Peery Park project area. 
Thus, the effect of the governmental decision concerning the Peery Park EIR and Specific 
Plan will have a foreseeable and material financial effect on your real property interest and you may 
not make, participate in making, or influence the decision.6 
Exception 
An official is not making, participating in making or influencing a governmental decision if 
he or she makes an appearance "as a member of the general public before an agency in the course of 
its prescribed governmental function to represent himself or herself on matters related solely to the 
4 You reference the Pittman Advice Letter, No. A• 14-167 for the apparent proposition that a change in the 
\'alue of real property, by itself, is not enough to find that a decision will ha\'e a material financial effect on the real 
property. But that mischaractenzes the facts and conclusions in the Pittmcm advice letter. There, pursuant to 
I 8702.2(a)( 11) (former Regulation I 8705.2(a)( 11 )), the Commission concluded there were sufficient facts, including a 
comprehensive appraisal, demonstrating that there would be no reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the 
official's property despite its location within 500 feet of the subject development. Indeed, the appraisal concluded that 
the proposed development would have no effect on the market value of the official's property. No facts exist in the 
present situation to make such a finding here. 
s Regulation I 8706(b) provides that "decisions are 'inextricably interrelated' when the result of one decision 
will effectively determine, affirm, nullify. or alter the result of another decision." 
6 This conclusion is consistent with the conclusions reached in three previous advice letters we issued 
concerning your invol\'ement in various matters related to the Peery Park development. (See Boc:o Advice Letter, No. 
A-13-078; Boco Advice Letter, No. A-13-092; and Boco Advice Letter, No. A-13-128.) 


File No. A-16-148 
Page No. 5 
official's personal interest . . .. " (Regulation 18704(d)(2).) The exception applies to real property 
"owned entirely by the official, members of his or her immediate family, or the official and 
members of his or her immediate family." (Regulation l 8704(d)(2)(A).) 
This exception is interpreted narrowly and does not allow an official to provide general 
comments regarding the matter. Care should be taken to clarify that the official is not acting in an 
official capacity. (McHug/i Advice Letter, No. 1-98-324; Gallagher Advice Letter, No. l-94-279 and 
Larsen Advice Letter, No. A-87-151.) Thus, the exception does not permit you to represent any 
other person's interests or the interests of any group. 
To qualify for the exception, an official must follow the procedures set forth in Regulation 
18707. The official must publicly identify each type of financial interest involved in the decision 
that gives rise to the disqualifying conflict of interest. The identification must be made after the 
announcement of the agenda item to be discussed or voted on, but before the discussion or vote 
commences. The official must also recuse himself or herself from voting on the matter, leave the 
dais to speak from the same area as the members of the public and limit his or her remarks solely to 
the official's personal interests. 
JW:jgl 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
Seni r Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question:
Do the Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit you from taking part in City Council 
decisions concerning the Peery Park Specific Plan and Final Environmental Impact Report based on 
your ownership of a residence in close proximity to the project area at issue?
sections.conclusion:
Yes. As described below, the Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibits you from taking 
part in City Council decisions concerning the Peery Park Specific Plan and Final Environmental 
Impact Report based on your ownership of a residence in close proximity to the project area at 
issue.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Do the Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit you from taking part in City Council 
decisions concerning the Peery Park Specific Plan and Final Environmental Impact Report based on 
your ownership of a residence in close proximity to the project area at issue?

CONCLUSION: Yes. As described below, the Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibits you from taking 
part in City Council decisions concerning the Peery Park Specific Plan and Final Environmental 
Impact Report based on your ownership of a residence in close proximity to the project area at 
issue.
